name: Dzongkha
notes: Dzongkha, national language of Bhutan, written in the Tibetan script (Uchen/Joyi), an abugida. The grapheme inventory is NOT yet specified. Tibetan-script orthography is deeply historical: the written stack (prefix, superscript, root, subscript, suffix, post-suffix letters) reflects Old Tibetan and maps to the modern Dzongkha pronunciation only through a large set of stack-level rules producing tone and vowel changes — it is not a letter-by-letter transcription. A correct spec needs the Roman Dzongkha transcription rules of van Driem's grammar; that research has not been done. Metadata only. PHONEME INVENTORY: attested inventory from PHOIBLE (glottocode dzon1239), declared directly. `graphemes` stays empty because none of that depends on the writing system: the sounds of this language are known regardless of whether — or how — it is written.

sources.author: Moran, Steven & McCloy, Daniel (eds.)
sources.title: PHOIBLE 2.0
sources.publisher: Max Planck Institute for the Science of Human History
sources.notes: Attested phoneme inventory for glottocode dzon1239.

ancestors.notes: Sino-Tibetan — the classification node this language sits under.
